drug_classes.label: Anticoagulants / antiplatelets
drug_classes.consumer_description: Medicines that thin the blood or reduce clotting.

drug_classes.label: Antidepressants (SSRIs / SNRIs / MAOIs)
drug_classes.consumer_description: Medicines for depression and anxiety that raise serotonin.

drug_classes.label: Cardiac glycosides
drug_classes.consumer_description: Medicines that strengthen and steady the heartbeat (narrow safety margin).

drug_classes.label: Diuretics (water tablets)
drug_classes.consumer_description: Medicines that increase urine output to remove excess fluid and lower blood pressure.

drug_classes.label: Hormonal therapies
drug_classes.consumer_description: Hormone medicines such as the contraceptive pill and hormone replacement.

drug_classes.label: Immunosuppressants
drug_classes.consumer_description: Medicines that dampen the immune system (e.g. after a transplant).

drug_classes.label: Medicines processed by CYP3A4
drug_classes.consumer_description: A large group of medicines broken down by a common liver enzyme; herbs that speed up or slow this enzyme can change their levels.

drug_classes.label: Sedatives / CNS depressants
drug_classes.consumer_description: Medicines that calm the nervous system or aid sleep.

drug_classes.label: Thyroid medicines
drug_classes.consumer_description: Medicines that replace or regulate thyroid hormone.

dangerous_plants.label: Agave
dangerous_plants.toxic_principle: Calcium oxalate raphides, steroidal saponins and acrid oils in the sap.
dangerous_plants.toxicity: A large rosette succulent whose sap is a skin and mucous-membrane irritant, not a soothing gel. Cutaneous exposure causes painful irritant contact dermatitis (with blistering and, in reported cases, purpura); contact with the mouth or gut burns and inflames. It forms a rosette very like an aloe and is easily mistaken for it, but no part is a safe aloe substitute.
dangerous_plants.onset: Burning and dermatitis within minutes to hours of skin or mucous-membrane contact

dangerous_plants.label: Angel wing
dangerous_plants.toxic_principle: An unstable amino-acid toxin (pleurocybellaziridine) affecting the brain
dangerous_plants.toxicity: A thin white bracket that grows on conifer wood and is easily taken for an oyster mushroom; linked to an outbreak of fatal brain inflammation (encephalopathy), especially in people with kidney impairment. Now regarded as unsafe to eat.
dangerous_plants.onset: Delayed (days to a few weeks in reported cases)

dangerous_plants.label: Autumn crocus (meadow saffron)
dangerous_plants.toxic_principle: Colchicine (an antimitotic alkaloid)
dangerous_plants.toxicity: There is no antidote; the strap-shaped spring leaves are mistaken for wild garlic and the corm/flower for saffron. Poisoning is deceptively delayed (2–12 h) then causes catastrophic vomiting, organ failure and death.
dangerous_plants.onset: Deceptively delayed (2–12 h) then multi-organ failure

dangerous_plants.label: Cherry laurel
dangerous_plants.toxic_principle: Cyanogenic glycosides (prunasin, prulaurasin, amygdalin) in the leaves and seeds; crushing, chewing or cooking releases hydrogen cyanide (HCN) via beta-glucosidase.
dangerous_plants.toxicity: The leaves and seeds are cyanogenic. When crushed, chewed or cooked they release hydrogen cyanide, which blocks the body's cells from using oxygen; poisoning can cause headache, dizziness, vomiting, breathlessness and convulsions, and severe cases (especially in children and livestock) can be fatal. The thick glossy leaves closely resemble edible bay/laurel and have been collected in mistake for it.
dangerous_plants.onset: Minutes to a few hours after ingesting or chewing crushed leaves

dangerous_plants.label: Cowbane (European water hemlock)
dangerous_plants.toxic_principle: Cicutoxin (a C17 polyacetylene convulsant)
dangerous_plants.toxicity: One of the most violently poisonous temperate plants; the chambered, sweet-smelling rootstock causes rapid onset of severe, recurrent seizures and can be fatal within hours.

dangerous_plants.label: Creeping buttercup
dangerous_plants.toxic_principle: Protoanemonin, a volatile vesicant lactone released from ranunculin when fresh tissue is crushed or chewed; destroyed on drying.
dangerous_plants.toxicity: Fresh sap and leaves are acrid and irritant. Skin contact can cause dermatitis and blistering, and chewing fresh material burns and inflames the mouth and gut (gastroenteritis). Rarely dangerous, because the toxin is destroyed on drying and the acrid, blistering taste discourages swallowing any quantity.
dangerous_plants.onset: Minutes to a few hours after contact with or ingestion of fresh material

dangerous_plants.label: Dwarf elder (danewort)
dangerous_plants.toxic_principle: Cyanogenic glycosides and ebulin (a ribosome-inactivating lectin)
dangerous_plants.toxicity: A herbaceous elder relative whose berries and foliage are markedly more toxic than true elder; ingestion causes severe nausea, vomiting and diarrhoea. Its flat-topped white flower clusters and dark berries are easily confused with elder.

dangerous_plants.label: Fool's parsley
dangerous_plants.toxic_principle: Polyacetylenes (aethusin, aethusanol) and related compounds
dangerous_plants.toxicity: A hemlock relative that mimics parsley and chervil; ingestion has caused burning of the mouth, nausea, vomiting and neurological symptoms, particularly in children — treat any parsley look-alike with a foul or garlicky smell as suspect.

dangerous_plants.label: Foxglove
dangerous_plants.toxic_principle: Cardiac glycosides (digitoxin, digoxin-like glycosides)
dangerous_plants.toxicity: All parts contain heart poisons; the non-flowering leaf rosette is easily mistaken for comfrey, mullein or borage, and ingestion causes nausea, disturbed colour vision and dangerous, potentially fatal heart-rhythm disturbances.

dangerous_plants.label: Funeral bell (deadly Galerina)
dangerous_plants.toxic_principle: Amatoxins (α-amanitin)
dangerous_plants.toxicity: Contains the same liver-destroying amatoxins as the death cap; a few of these small brown mushrooms can kill. Symptoms are deceptively delayed (6–24 h) — vomiting and diarrhoea, apparent recovery, then catastrophic liver failure.

dangerous_plants.label: Giant hogweed
dangerous_plants.toxic_principle: Furanocoumarins (furocoumarins, e.g. psoralens)
dangerous_plants.toxicity: The clear, watery sap makes skin hypersensitive to sunlight; contact followed by sun exposure causes severe blistering burns (phytophotodermatitis) and long-lasting scars, and sap in the eye can damage sight. Not a poison by ingestion but a serious contact hazard for anyone gathering tall umbellifers.
dangerous_plants.onset: Skin reaction develops 15 min–2 h after sun exposure; blisters peak 2–3 days later

dangerous_plants.label: Hairy curtain crust
dangerous_plants.toxic_principle: None established (inedible, not regarded as poisonous)
dangerous_plants.toxicity: Not a poison, but a common "false turkey tail" that is confused with turkey tail (Trametes versicolor); it has a smooth to wrinkled underside with NO pores, so it lacks the medicinal properties sought and wastes a forager's effort.

dangerous_plants.label: Hemlock water-dropwort
dangerous_plants.toxic_principle: Oenanthotoxin (a C17 polyacetylene convulsant, akin to cicutoxin)
dangerous_plants.toxicity: Among the most poisonous plants in Britain; the fleshy, parsnip-like roots ("dead man's fingers") are intensely toxic and cause vomiting, violent convulsions and death; often smells pleasant, which disarms foragers.

dangerous_plants.label: Jack-o'-lantern mushroom
dangerous_plants.toxic_principle: Illudin S and related sesquiterpenes
dangerous_plants.toxicity: A clustered orange wood-rotting mushroom with true, sharp-edged gills (that glow faintly in the dark); eating it causes severe vomiting and cramps, though it is rarely fatal. Confused with chanterelles and, when growing on wood, with oyster mushrooms.

dangerous_plants.label: Lily-of-the-valley
dangerous_plants.toxic_principle: Cardiac glycosides (convallatoxin, convalloside)
dangerous_plants.toxicity: Whole plant is poisonous; its broad green spring leaves are notoriously confused with wild garlic (ramsons) before flowering, and ingestion can cause vomiting and serious, potentially fatal heart-rhythm disturbances.

dangerous_plants.label: Lords-and-ladies (cuckoo-pint)
dangerous_plants.toxic_principle: Insoluble calcium-oxalate raphides (needle crystals)
dangerous_plants.toxicity: Biting into any part fires microscopic needle crystals into the mouth and throat, causing intense burning, swelling and blistering; severe swelling can threaten the airway. The arrow-shaped spring leaves grow among ramsons and other foraged greens.

dangerous_plants.label: Petty spurge
dangerous_plants.toxic_principle: Diterpene esters (ingenol mebutate) in the milky latex
dangerous_plants.toxicity: Every part exudes a caustic milky latex that severely irritates and blisters skin and, in the eye, can cause serious injury; ingestion burns the mouth and gut. It grows as a weed among low salad greens such as chickweed.

dangerous_plants.label: Poison fire coral
dangerous_plants.toxic_principle: Macrocyclic trichothecene mycotoxins (satratoxin H and related satratoxins, roridins, verrucarol) that inhibit protein synthesis and cell division.
dangerous_plants.toxicity: One of the few deadly-poisonous fungi. Its bright red, antler- or coral-like fruit bodies contain macrocyclic trichothecenes; even a small amount taken as food or tea causes early gastrointestinal upset followed by delayed multi-organ failure - pancytopenia, skin desquamation, liver and kidney failure and disseminated intravascular coagulation - and has repeatedly caused death. In its immature stage it resembles the medicinal fungi Ganoderma lucidum (reishi/lingzhi) and Cordyceps militaris and has been collected in mistake for them. Occurs in East Asia (Korea, Japan, China) and has been reported spreading elsewhere.
dangerous_plants.onset: Early gastrointestinal symptoms within hours; delayed pancytopenia and organ failure over days

dangerous_plants.label: Poison hemlock
dangerous_plants.toxic_principle: Coniine and γ-coniceine (piperidine alkaloids)
dangerous_plants.toxicity: Ingestion causes burning of the mouth, salivation, muscle weakness and an ascending paralysis that can stop breathing; a few leaves or seeds can be fatal, and the mind stays clear as paralysis sets in.

dangerous_plants.label: Savin juniper
dangerous_plants.toxic_principle: Podophyllotoxin (an antimitotic cell poison) plus the essential-oil terpenes sabinene and sabinol; sabinol is a uterine stimulant (abortifacient).
dangerous_plants.toxicity: A low, spreading ornamental juniper that is genuinely poisonous, unlike edible common juniper. Its foliage and shoots contain podophyllotoxin and irritant terpenes; ingestion causes violent gastrointestinal irritation, kidney damage and, taken historically as an abortifacient, severe poisoning and deaths. Modern guidance advises against any internal use. It is widely planted in gardens and parks, so 'juniper berries' picked from landscaping can be this toxic species.
dangerous_plants.onset: Gastrointestinal and systemic symptoms within hours of ingestion

dangerous_plants.label: Scarlet pimpernel
dangerous_plants.toxic_principle: Triterpenoid saponins (e.g. anagalligenin/cyclamin-type)
dangerous_plants.toxicity: A low sprawling weed of the same habitats as chickweed; the plant is bitter and mildly toxic (saponins), causing gastrointestinal upset, and has caused poisoning in grazing animals. Its square-ish stems and orange-red flowers distinguish it.

dangerous_plants.label: White hellebore (false hellebore)
dangerous_plants.toxic_principle: Steroidal alkaloids (protoveratrines, veratridine, jervine)
dangerous_plants.toxicity: The classic and repeatedly fatal mix-up for foragers gathering yellow gentian; the root and early rosette cause vomiting, a dangerously slow heartbeat and collapse in blood pressure within hours.

dangerous_plants.label: Yellow flag iris
dangerous_plants.toxic_principle: Pentacyclic terpenoids (zeorin, missourin, missouriensin) and irritant resinous glycosides, concentrated in the rhizome.
dangerous_plants.toxicity: A low-severity poison of wet ground. All parts, especially the rhizome, cause salivation, vomiting, drooling and diarrhoea if eaten, and the sap can irritate the skin. Its erect sword-shaped leaves are odourless and closely resemble those of aromatic sweet flag, whose prized rhizome it is confused with; the scientific name pseudacorus means 'false sweet-flag'.
dangerous_plants.onset: Salivation and gastrointestinal symptoms within hours of ingestion

dangerous_plants.label: Yew
dangerous_plants.toxic_principle: Cardiotoxic taxine alkaloids (taxine A and B) present in all parts except the fleshy red aril, plus the marker 3,5-dimethoxyphenol; taxines block cardiac sodium and calcium channels.
dangerous_plants.toxicity: One of the most poisonous trees in Europe. All parts except the red flesh of the berry-like aril contain taxine alkaloids; ingestion of even a few leaves can cause vomiting, dizziness, dilated pupils, then life-threatening heart-rhythm disturbances, seizures and sudden cardiac arrest, and has caused many deaths. There is no specific antidote. The flat evergreen needles and red berry-like arils are mistaken for edible conifers such as pine.
dangerous_plants.onset: Symptoms within 30 minutes to a few hours; cardiac collapse can be rapid
